description: Lists files and folders in the current (or specified) directory.
tip: In Linux, files beginning with a dot (.) are hidden. Use ls -a to show them — there's no separate hidden-file flag like Windows.

options.desc: Long + hidden — the most useful combo

example: # Windows equivalent: dir ls # basic listing ls -la # detailed listing including hidden files ls -lh /home/user # human-readable sizes, specific path
gotcha: Linux filenames are case-sensitive! 'File.txt' and 'file.txt' are two different files.

description: Navigates between directories. Same command name, but with important differences in path notation.
tip: Linux uses forward slashes (/) instead of backslashes. The home directory has a shortcut: the tilde (~). Typing just cd with no argument always takes you home.

options.desc: Go up one level (same as Windows)
Go back to previous directory (very handy!)
Go to the root of the filesystem

example: # Windows: cd C:\Users\John\Documents cd /home/john/Documents # absolute path cd Documents # relative path cd .. # go up one folder cd ~ # go home instantly cd - # toggle back to last directory

description: Copies files or entire directory trees to another location.
tip: To copy a directory and all its contents, you must use cp -r (recursive). Forgetting -r is one of the most common beginner mistakes.

options.desc: Only copy if source is newer

example: # Windows: copy file.txt backup.txt cp file.txt backup.txt # copy a file cp file.txt /home/user/docs/ # copy to another directory # Windows: xcopy /s /e MyFolder Backup cp -r MyFolder Backup # copy an entire folder cp -rv MyFolder /backup/ # copy folder, show progress
gotcha: cp will silently overwrite existing files by default. Use cp -i to be prompted before overwriting.

description: Moves files to a new location, or renames them. In Linux, renaming IS moving.
tip: In Linux there's no separate rename command — you just use mv with a different destination name. This works for both files and directories.

options.desc: Move only if source is newer

example: # Windows: move file.txt Documents\ mv file.txt Documents/ # move a file # Windows: rename oldname.txt newname.txt mv oldname.txt newname.txt # rename a file mv OldFolder NewFolder # rename a directory mv -v *.log /var/log/archive/ # move all .log files

description: Removes files or directories. Be very careful — Linux has no Recycle Bin by default.
tip: There is no Recycle Bin in the Linux terminal. Deleted files are gone immediately. Always double-check your command, especially when using wildcards.
example: # Windows: del file.txt rm file.txt # delete a file rm -i *.tmp # delete with confirmation prompt # Windows: rmdir /s /q MyFolder rm -r MyFolder # delete folder and all contents rm -rf MyFolder # force delete (no prompts)
gotcha: NEVER run 'rm -rf /' — it deletes your entire system with no undo. Always review commands with wildcards or -rf before pressing Enter.

description: Creates one or more new directories. Same name, but with a useful extra flag.
tip: The -p flag creates the full path in one shot, including all missing parent directories. This is far more convenient than Windows' mkdir.
example: # Windows: mkdir MyFolder mkdir MyFolder # create a folder # Windows requires creating each level separately mkdir -p Projects/Web/MyApp/src # create full path at once! mkdir -p ~/Documents/Work/Reports # using home shortcut

description: Shows the contents of a text file in the terminal. Also used to combine files.
tip: cat stands for concatenate. You can use it to join multiple files together with cat file1 file2 > combined.txt.

options.desc: Scroll through large files (better than cat)

example: # Windows: type file.txt cat file.txt # display file cat -n file.txt # display with line numbers # For large files, use 'less' instead: less bigfile.txt # scroll with arrows, q to quit head -n 10 file.txt # first 10 lines tail -f /var/log/syslog # watch a log file in real-time

description: Displays IP addresses, network interfaces, and connection details.
tip: The old command ifconfig still works on many systems but is deprecated. The modern replacement is ip addr. Network interfaces are named differently too — eth0 for wired, wlan0 for wireless.

options.desc: Quick — just show your IP addresses

example: # Windows: ipconfig ip addr # show all interfaces ip addr show wlan0 # show wireless interface # Windows: ipconfig /all ip addr # ip addr already shows full detail nmcli # NetworkManager CLI (very detailed) # Quick IP check: hostname -I # just the IP addresses, nothing else

description: Sends ICMP echo requests to test if a host is reachable. Same name, but runs forever by default on Linux.
tip: On Windows, ping sends 4 packets then stops. On Linux, ping runs forever until you press Ctrl+C. Use -c 4 to match Windows behavior.
example: # Windows: ping google.com (sends 4 packets, stops) ping google.com # runs FOREVER — press Ctrl+C to stop ping -c 4 google.com # send exactly 4 packets, then stop ping -c 4 192.168.1.1 # ping your router by IP
gotcha: On Linux, ping runs indefinitely. Always use -c N to limit packet count, or press Ctrl+C to stop it.

description: Shows each network hop between your machine and a destination, helping diagnose where connections slow down or fail.
tip: Linux also has tracepath which doesn't require root and is often pre-installed. For a combined ping+trace tool, try mtr — it's fantastic.

options.desc: Similar but doesn't need root access

example: # Windows: tracert google.com traceroute google.com # trace the route traceroute -n google.com # faster, skip DNS lookups tracepath google.com # alternative, no root needed mtr google.com # live updating trace (best tool)

description: Displays active network connections, listening ports, and socket statistics.
tip: While netstat still works on Linux, ss is the modern replacement — it's faster and shows more information.

options.desc: Show which process owns each socket

example: # Windows: netstat -an ss -tuln # show all listening TCP/UDP ports ss -tulnp # include the process name # Windows: netstat -b (show processes) ss -tp # TCP connections with process info

description: Scans and repairs filesystem errors on a disk or partition.
tip: Never run fsck on a mounted drive — you must unmount it first or boot from a live USB. Linux usually auto-checks disks on boot if needed.

options.desc: Dry run — check only, make no changes
Automatically answer yes to all fixes

example: # Windows: chkdsk C: /f # First, unmount the drive (REQUIRED): umount /dev/sdb1 fsck /dev/sdb1 # check the partition fsck -y /dev/sdb1 # auto-fix all errors # Check your root partition — needs live USB or: sudo touch /forcefsck # force check on next reboot
gotcha: Running fsck on a mounted filesystem can cause severe data corruption. Always unmount first.

description: Creates a new filesystem on a disk partition, erasing all existing data.
tip: Linux supports many filesystem types. ext4 is the most common for Linux drives. Use vfat or exfat if you need the drive to work on Windows too.

options.desc: List all drives first to find the right one

example: # Windows: format D: /fs:NTFS /q # First identify your drive safely: lsblk # list all drives and partitions # Format (REPLACES ALL DATA on that device!): mkfs.ext4 /dev/sdb1 # Linux-native format mkfs.vfat -F 32 /dev/sdb1 # FAT32 for USB sticks
gotcha: Double-check the device path (/dev/sdb1, etc.) before formatting. Formatting the wrong drive permanently destroys its data.

description: Creates, deletes, and modifies disk partitions. Use with extreme care.
tip: fdisk is interactive (like diskpart). parted supports GPT disks and is better for modern systems. lsblk and fdisk -l let you safely view partitions first.

options.desc: Safe read-only view of all disks
List all partitions on all disks

example: # List disks safely first: lsblk # visual tree of disks & partitions sudo fdisk -l # detailed partition info # Interactive partition editor: sudo fdisk /dev/sdb # opens interactive mode m = help menu p = print partition table n = new partition d = delete partition w = write and exit
gotcha: Partition changes only take effect when you write them (w command). But once written, data loss is immediate. Use lsblk first to confirm the correct disk.

description: Shows all currently running processes, like the Processes tab in Task Manager.
tip: The ps aux format is the most commonly used. For an interactive Task Manager, type htop — it's much nicer (install with sudo apt install htop).

options.desc: Built-in interactive monitor (press q to quit)

example: # Windows: tasklist ps aux # all processes ps aux | grep firefox # find firefox process ps aux --sort=-%mem # sort by memory usage # Interactive (like Task Manager): top # built-in, press q to exit htop # nicer version, install first

description: Ends a running process by its process ID (PID) or name.
tip: Use ps aux | grep appname to find the PID first. kill -9 is the force kill — it can't be ignored by the process, like End Task in Windows.
example: # Windows: taskkill /PID 1234 /F # Step 1: Find the process ID ps aux | grep firefox john 4892 ... firefox # Step 2: Kill it kill 4892 # graceful stop (tries nicely first) kill -9 4892 # force kill if graceful fails # Kill by name: killall firefox # kill all firefox processes

description: Clears all text from the terminal window.
tip: You can also press Ctrl+L — it's the keyboard shortcut that works in almost every Linux terminal and is often faster than typing clear.
example: # Windows: cls clear # clears the screen # Or just press: Ctrl + L # same effect, much faster # If the terminal looks garbled: reset # completely resets terminal state

description: Displays a line of text. Also used in scripts for variable output.
tip: Use echo $VARIABLE to display environment variables. Double quotes preserve spacing; single quotes prevent variable expansion — a subtle but important difference.
example: # Windows: echo Hello World echo "Hello World" # print text echo $HOME # print home directory path echo $USER # print current username echo "Done!" > log.txt # write text to a file echo "More" >> log.txt # append to a file

description: Lists or modifies environment variables available in the current session.
tip: Variables set with export are only available for that session. To make them permanent, add the export line to ~/.bashrc or ~/.profile.

options.desc: Set a variable for this session

example: # Windows: set (list all variables) env # list all environment variables printenv HOME # show specific variable # Windows: set MYVAR=hello export MYVAR="hello" # set for this session echo $MYVAR # use it # Permanent — add to ~/.bashrc: echo 'export MYVAR="hello"' >> ~/.bashrc

description: Searches for files matching given criteria anywhere in the filesystem.
tip: Linux's find is much more powerful than Windows' — you can search by name, size, date, permissions, and even execute commands on results.

options.desc: Search by filename in current dir

example: # Windows: dir /s /b *.txt find . -name "*.txt" # find all .txt files here find /home -name "report.pdf" # search in /home find . -name "*.log" -mtime -1 # logs from last 24hrs find . -size +100M # files over 100MB # Find and delete (use with care!): find . -name "*.tmp" -delete # delete all .tmp files

description: Searches for text patterns inside files. One of the most-used Linux commands.
tip: grep supports regular expressions, making it far more powerful than Windows' findstr. The pipe operator | lets you chain it with other commands.

options.desc: Show lines that do NOT match

example: # Windows: findstr "error" logfile.txt grep "error" logfile.txt # search for 'error' grep -i "error" logfile.txt # case-insensitive grep -rn "TODO" ~/Projects/ # find TODOs in all files # Super useful: pipe into grep: ps aux | grep nginx # find nginx process cat access.log | grep "404" # find all 404 errors

description: Shows OS version, hardware details, memory, and system configuration.
tip: Linux has several tools for system info. uname -a is quick for OS/kernel info. lshw gives detailed hardware info. neofetch is popular for a nice summary display.
example: # Windows: systeminfo uname -a # kernel & OS version lscpu # CPU details free -h # RAM info df -h # disk space lsb_release -a # Linux distro info sudo lshw # full hardware inventory

description: Powers off, reboots, or schedules a shutdown of the Linux system.
tip: Linux's shutdown command is flexible — you can schedule it minutes in advance and cancel it. The now keyword shuts down immediately.
example: # Windows: shutdown /s /t 0 shutdown now # power off immediately poweroff # same thing, shorter # Windows: shutdown /r /t 0 shutdown -r now # reboot now reboot # same thing, shorter # Schedule for later: shutdown -h +30 # shutdown in 30 minutes shutdown -c # cancel scheduled shutdown

description: Displays the username of the currently logged-in user.
tip: Also try id for more detail including your group memberships, which matter a lot for Linux permissions.

options.desc: Show user ID, group IDs, and names
List which groups you belong to
Show who is logged in and what they're doing

example: # Windows: whoami whoami # prints: john (your username) id # uid=1000(john) gid=1000(john) groups=... groups # list your group memberships w # who's logged in across all sessions

description: Executes a command with elevated privileges (as root/superuser).
tip: sudo stands for "superuser do". It's like Windows UAC — you get elevated permissions for one command. sudo su or sudo -i opens a root shell (use sparingly).

options.desc: List what you're allowed to sudo

example: # Windows: runas /user:Administrator cmd.exe sudo apt update # update package list (needs root) sudo systemctl restart nginx # restart a service # Forgot sudo? Re-run last command with sudo: sudo !! # very useful shortcut # Open root shell: sudo -i # root shell (prompt changes to #)
gotcha: Don't stay in a root shell longer than needed. It's easy to accidentally damage your system. Use sudo per-command instead.

description: Renames a file or batch-renames multiple files matching a pattern.
tip: Single file renaming uses mv. For batch renaming (e.g. change all .txt to .bak), use the rename utility which supports Perl-style patterns.
example: # Windows: ren report.txt report.bak mv report.txt report.bak # rename one file # Windows: ren *.txt *.bak (batch) rename 's/.txt/.bak/' *.txt # batch rename rename -n 's/report/summary/' * # preview changes first

description: Controls who can read, write, or execute a file. Linux uses a more powerful permission model than Windows.
tip: Linux permissions use octal numbers (755, 644) or symbolic notation (u+x). The pattern is: Owner / Group / Everyone. 755 = owner can do everything, group and others can read/execute.
example: # Windows: attrib +r file.txt (set read-only) chmod 444 file.txt # read-only for everyone chmod 644 file.txt # owner can write, others read-only # Make a script executable: chmod +x myscript.sh # allow it to be run ./myscript.sh # then run it like this # Change ownership: chown john:john file.txt # set owner to john chown -R john /var/www/ # recursive ownership change
gotcha: Avoid setting permissions to 777 (anyone can do anything) — it's a security risk, especially on web servers.

description: Copies directories with more control than cp — supports sync, progress display, resume, and remote copying.
tip: rsync only copies changed files, making it far faster than xcopy for large backups. It can also copy over SSH to remote machines.

options.desc: Delete files in dest not in source (true sync)

example: # Windows: xcopy /s /e /i Source Dest rsync -av Source/ Dest/ # copy directory rsync -av --progress Source/ Dest/ # show progress # Sync (delete files removed from source): rsync -av --delete Source/ Dest/ # mirror source to dest # Backup to a remote server over SSH: rsync -avz ~/Documents/ user@server:/backup/
gotcha: Note the trailing slash: 'rsync -av src/ dest/' copies the *contents* of src. Without the slash, it copies the folder itself inside dest.

description: Shows the directory structure as a visual tree. May need to be installed on Linux.
tip: tree may not be installed by default. Run sudo apt install tree. As an alternative, find . | sort | sed can approximate it without installing anything.
example: # Windows: tree /f /a tree # show current directory tree tree -L 2 # limit to 2 levels deep tree -d /home/user # directories only # Install if missing: sudo apt install tree # Debian/Ubuntu sudo dnf install tree # Fedora/RHEL

description: Saves your current directory to a stack so you can jump back to it later after navigating elsewhere.
tip: These commands work the same in Linux! pushd saves your spot, popd returns you to it. dirs shows the full directory stack.

options.desc: Save current dir and go to path

example: # Save your place, go deep, come back pushd /var/log/nginx # go there, save current dir (do some work...) popd # return to where you were # Stack multiple directories: pushd ~/Projects pushd /etc dirs -v # shows: 0=/etc 1=~/Projects 2=... popd # back to ~/Projects

description: Displays large text files one screen at a time, allowing you to scroll up and down.
tip: less is better than more because you can scroll both forwards AND backwards. The name is a joke: 'less is more than more'. Press q to quit, / to search.
example: # Windows: more bigfile.txt less bigfile.txt # open file (scroll with arrows) less /var/log/syslog # great for reading log files # Pipe output into less: ps aux | less # scroll through long output grep -r "error" . | less # Inside less: press / then type to search

description: Queries DNS servers to resolve hostnames to IP addresses and inspect DNS records.
tip: nslookup works on Linux too, but dig gives much more detail and is preferred by administrators. It's in the dnsutils package.

options.desc: Just the IP, no extra info

example: # Windows: nslookup google.com nslookup google.com # works just like Windows dig google.com # detailed DNS info dig +short google.com # just the IP address dig google.com MX # who handles Google's email dig -x 8.8.8.8 # what host is 8.8.8.8?

description: Transfers files between computers over a network. Linux has several options with different security levels.
tip: Avoid plain FTP — it sends passwords in cleartext. Use scp or sftp (SSH-based, encrypted) whenever possible. scp works like a remote cp.
example: # Windows: ftp server.com # Upload a file securely: scp report.pdf john@server.com:/home/john/ # Download a file: scp john@server.com:/var/log/app.log . # Interactive session: sftp john@server.com get remotefile.txt # download put localfile.txt # upload bye # exit
gotcha: Plain FTP transmits your username and password in plain text. Never use it over the internet — use SFTP or SCP instead.

description: Mounts a network share (Windows SMB/CIFS share or NFS) so you can access it like a local folder.
tip: To mount Windows shared folders (SMB), you need the cifs-utils package. The share then appears as a regular directory in your filesystem.
example: # Windows: net use Z: \\server\share # Install SMB support: sudo apt install cifs-utils # Mount a Windows share: sudo mount -t cifs //192.168.1.10/SharedDocs /mnt/share -o username=john # Browse without mounting: smbclient //server/share -U john

description: Lists, connects to, and manages wireless network profiles.
tip: nmcli is the command-line interface for NetworkManager, which most desktop Linux distros use. It handles both Wi-Fi and wired connections.

options.flag: nmcli dev wifi connect SSID password PASS

options.desc: Older Wi-Fi info command (may be installed)

example: # Windows: netsh wlan show profiles nmcli dev wifi list # scan for networks nmcli connection show # saved profiles # Connect to a network: nmcli dev wifi connect "MyNetwork" password "mypassword" # Disconnect: nmcli dev disconnect wlan0

description: Installs programs and tools from an online repository — like an App Store for the terminal.
tip: Linux package managers are one of its best features. No hunting for installers online — just type the package name and it installs, along with any dependencies automatically.

options.desc: Search for a package by name

example: # Windows: winget install vlc sudo apt install vlc # install VLC media player sudo apt install htop curl git # install multiple at once # Search before installing: apt search video player # find packages apt show vlc # read about it first

description: Removes installed packages from the system.
tip: apt remove removes the program but leaves config files. apt purge removes everything including config files. Use purge for a clean uninstall.
example: # Windows: winget uninstall vlc sudo apt remove vlc # uninstall VLC sudo apt purge vlc # uninstall + remove config files # Clean up orphaned packages: sudo apt autoremove # remove no-longer-needed deps

short: Update the system and all software
description: Downloads and installs updates for the OS and all installed packages.
linuxFull: sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade
tip: apt update refreshes the list of available packages — it doesn't install anything yet. apt upgrade then downloads and installs the actual updates.
example: # Windows: Windows Update (GUI) # The classic two-step update: sudo apt update # step 1: refresh package list sudo apt upgrade # step 2: install updates # Do both in one line: sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y # Check what would be updated: apt list --upgradable

short: Find where a command is installed
description: Locates the full path of an executable command or program.
tip: which shows just the path of the executable. whereis also finds man pages and source files. Useful for knowing which version of a program will run.

options.desc: Binary, man page, and source paths
Shows if it's an alias, function, or binary
Script-safe way to check if cmd exists

example: # Windows: where python which python3 # /usr/bin/python3 which git # /usr/bin/git whereis nginx # binary + man page locations type ls # ls is aliased to 'ls --color=auto'

description: Starts, stops, restarts, and checks the status of background services (like Windows Services).
tip: systemctl is the main tool for managing services in modern Linux (systemd). It replaces older commands like service and init.d.
example: # Windows: net start/stop "Service Name" sudo systemctl start nginx # start nginx web server sudo systemctl stop nginx # stop it sudo systemctl restart nginx # restart systemctl status nginx # is it running? # Auto-start on boot (like Windows service startup type): sudo systemctl enable nginx # enable auto-start sudo systemctl disable nginx # disable auto-start

description: Reads logs from the systemd journal — the Linux equivalent of Windows Event Viewer.
tip: journalctl -f follows the log in real-time like tail -f. Use -u servicename to filter logs for just one service.
example: # Windows: eventvwr (GUI only) journalctl -f # follow live system log journalctl -u ssh # SSH service logs only journalctl -b -p err # errors since last boot journalctl --since "2024-01-15" # logs from a specific date

short: List all services and their status
description: Shows all installed services and whether they are running, stopped, or failed.
tip: Use --state=failed to quickly see what's broken. The systemctl list-unit-files command shows which services are enabled to start at boot.
example: # Windows: sc query type= all systemctl list-units --type=service # running services systemctl list-units --state=failed # what's broken? systemctl list-unit-files # all + boot status # Filter by name: systemctl list-units --type=service | grep ssh

description: Adds new users, changes user properties, and manages user accounts on the system.
tip: Use adduser (note: no dash) instead of useradd on Debian/Ubuntu — it's friendlier and sets up the home directory interactively.

options.desc: Delete user and their home directory

example: # Windows: net user john Password1 /add sudo adduser john # create user (interactive) sudo passwd john # set/change password # Add to sudo group (give admin rights): sudo usermod -aG sudo john # Ubuntu/Debian sudo usermod -aG wheel john # Fedora/RHEL # Delete user: sudo userdel -r john # remove user + home dir

description: Creates groups, adds users to groups, and lists group memberships.
tip: Groups are central to Linux security. Adding a user to a group (like docker or sudo) grants them extra permissions without giving full root access.

options.desc: List groups a user belongs to
Add user to group (-a means append)
List all groups on the system

example: # Windows: net localgroup Administrators john /add groups john # what groups is john in? sudo groupadd developers # create a new group sudo usermod -aG developers john # add john to it # Allow john to run Docker without sudo: sudo usermod -aG docker john (log out and back in for changes to take effect)
gotcha: The -a flag in usermod -aG means 'append'. Without it, the user is removed from ALL other groups. Always use -aG together.

description: Sets or changes a user account password.
tip: Running passwd with no arguments changes your own password. With sudo passwd username an admin can change anyone's password.

options.desc: Force password change on next login

example: # Windows: net user john * (prompts for new password) passwd # change your own password sudo passwd john # admin: change john's password sudo passwd -l john # lock account (can't log in) sudo passwd -u john # unlock account

description: Sorts the lines of a text file or input alphabetically, numerically, or by other criteria.
tip: Linux sort is much more powerful than Windows. Combine with uniq to remove duplicate lines.
example: # Windows: sort file.txt sort file.txt # alphabetical sort sort -r file.txt # reverse order sort -n numbers.txt # numeric sort sort -u file.txt # sort + remove duplicates # Sort and pipe: cat names.txt | sort | uniq # unique sorted names ls -la | sort -k 5 -n # sort dir listing by file size

description: Compares two files and shows the differences between them line by line.
tip: diff output can look cryptic at first. Use diff -u for unified format — lines starting with - were removed, + were added. This is the same format used by git.
example: # Windows: fc file1.txt file2.txt diff file1.txt file2.txt # show differences diff -u file1.txt file2.txt # unified format (git-style) diff -r dir1/ dir2/ # compare two directories # Side-by-side view: diff --side-by-side file1.txt file2.txt

description: Counts lines, words, and characters in a file or piped input. Very useful in scripts.
tip: No direct Windows equivalent. wc -l is incredibly useful for counting results — e.g. how many log entries match a pattern.
example: # No direct Windows equivalent wc -l file.txt # how many lines? wc -w document.txt # word count # Super useful with pipes: ps aux | wc -l # how many processes running? grep "ERROR" app.log | wc -l # count errors in a log ls *.txt | wc -l # how many txt files?

description: Pipes command output directly into the clipboard so you can paste it elsewhere.
tip: You need to install xclip or xsel first. On Wayland (newer desktops) use wl-copy instead. Many people add a shell alias to make this easier.
example: # Windows: command | clip # Install first: sudo apt install xclip # Copy output to clipboard: cat file.txt | xclip -selection clipboard pwd | xclip -selection clipboard # copy current path # Handy alias — add to ~/.bashrc: alias clip='xclip -selection clipboard' # Then just: cat file.txt | clip

description: Runs commands or scripts automatically on a schedule — like Windows Task Scheduler.
tip: Cron uses a 5-field time format: minute hour day month weekday. The site crontab.guru is excellent for building cron expressions.

options.desc: Run once on every system startup

example: # Cron format: min hour day month weekday command crontab -e # open editor to add/edit jobs # Example cron entries: 0 9 * * 1-5 /home/john/backup.sh # 9am Mon-Fri */15 * * * * /usr/bin/check.sh # every 15 min 0 0 * * * /home/john/daily.sh # midnight daily @reboot /home/john/startup.sh # on every boot crontab -l # view your scheduled jobs
gotcha: Cron jobs run with a minimal environment — no PATH, no aliases. Always use full paths to commands in cron jobs (e.g. /usr/bin/python3, not just python3).

description: Displays detailed hardware inventory — CPU, RAM, motherboard, GPU, and more.
tip: inxi is the most user-friendly tool and formats output beautifully. Install it with sudo apt install inxi. Use -Fz for full info with privacy masking.
example: # Windows: msinfo32 (GUI) inxi -Fz # full system summary sudo lshw -short # compact hardware list lspci # graphics card, network card, etc. lsusb # USB devices lscpu # CPU architecture details

description: Shows real-time statistics on CPU, memory, disk I/O, and system activity.
tip: For an all-in-one performance monitor like Windows Resource Monitor, install glances — it shows CPU, memory, disk, and network in one terminal view.

options.desc: Per-process disk I/O (like Task Manager I/O tab)

example: # Windows: perfmon / Resource Monitor (GUI) vmstat 1 # live stats every second iostat -x 1 # disk read/write stats sudo iotop # which process is using disk? # All-in-one (install first): sudo apt install glances glances # best overall performance view

short: Display or set date and time
description: Shows the current date and time, or sets the system clock.
tip: date supports powerful format strings. date +%Y-%m-%d prints the date in a format safe for filenames — no spaces or slashes.
example: # Windows: date /t and time /t date # Sat Jan 15 14:32:00 EST 2025 date +"%Y-%m-%d" # 2025-01-15 date +"%Y%m%d_%H%M%S" # 20250115_143200 (for filenames!) timedatectl # timezone, NTP sync status # Use in filenames: tar -czf backup_$(date +%Y%m%d).tar.gz ~/Documents

description: Shows previously run commands. Linux keeps a much longer history than Windows.
tip: Press Ctrl+R to search your command history interactively — type part of a past command and it appears. This is one of the most powerful terminal productivity shortcuts.

options.desc: Show all recent commands with numbers

example: # Windows: doskey /history history # show all history history 20 # last 20 commands history | grep apt # find past apt commands # Re-run a command by number: 42 sudo apt update !42 # runs command 42 again # Best shortcut: Ctrl + R # then type to search history

description: Creates short nicknames for long commands. Add them to ~/.bashrc to make them permanent.
tip: Aliases are one of the best ways to personalise Linux. Common examples: alias ll='ls -la', alias update='sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y'.

options.desc: Create an alias for this session
Add aliases here to make them permanent

example: # No direct Windows equivalent (doskey macros are similar) # Create useful aliases: alias ll='ls -la' alias cls='clear' # feel at home! alias update='sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y' alias myip='curl ifconfig.me' # Make permanent — add to ~/.bashrc: echo "alias ll='ls -la'" >> ~/.bashrc source ~/.bashrc # reload without restarting terminal

description: Opens a file, folder, or URL with the appropriate default application — like double-clicking in the file manager.
tip: xdg-open works on any Linux desktop. It respects your default app settings. Works great for opening PDFs, images, URLs, and folders from the terminal.

options.desc: Open current directory in file manager

example: # Windows: start file.pdf xdg-open document.pdf # open in default PDF viewer xdg-open . # open current folder in Nautilus/Dolphin xdg-open https://google.com # open in Firefox/Chrome xdg-open photo.jpg # open in image viewer

description: Stops a currently running command or script. Ctrl+C sends SIGINT to the foreground process.
tip: Press Ctrl+C to interrupt any running command. Ctrl+Z suspends it (pauses it and puts it in the background). Ctrl+D sends end-of-file, which exits shells and many interactive programs.
example: # Stop a running command: Ctrl+C # Suspend ping, then resume: ping google.com Ctrl+Z bg # resume in background fg # bring back to foreground

short: Pause a script and wait for keypress
description: Halts a script and waits for the user to press a key before continuing. Used frequently in batch files.
tip: Linux has no built-in pause command. Use read in scripts to wait for input. For a single keypress without needing Enter, use read -n 1.
example: # Windows: pause read -p 'Press Enter to continue...' # Wait for any key (no Enter needed): read -n 1 -s -p 'Press any key...' echo # newline after # Auto-continue after timeout: read -t 10 -p 'Auto-continuing in 10s... ' || true

description: Adds human-readable comments to shell scripts that are ignored during execution.
tip: In bash, # comments out the rest of the line. The first line of every script should be a shebang: #!/bin/bash — this tells the OS which interpreter to use.
example: #!/bin/bash # This is a comment # Author: John Smith # Run backup ./backup.sh # inline comment

short: Exit the shell or return an exit code
description: Closes the current shell session or exits a script, optionally with a numeric return code.
tip: Exit codes matter in Linux. 0 = success, anything else = failure. Use $? to check the exit code of the last command. Scripts should always exit with a meaningful code.

options.desc: Check exit code of last command
Exit script automatically on any error

example: # Windows: exit 0 exit # close terminal / end script exit 0 # success exit 1 # failure # Check if last command succeeded: ls /tmp echo $? # 0 = success # Error-safe script header: #!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail

description: Tests a condition and executes different code depending on whether it is true or false.
tip: Use [[ ]] (double brackets) in bash — it's more powerful than [ ] and handles edge cases better. Always quote variables: "$var" not $var.
example: # Windows: if exist file.txt echo yes if [ -f "/etc/hosts" ]; then echo "File exists" fi # if/else: if [ "$USER" = "root" ]; then echo "Running as root" else echo "Normal user" fi
gotcha: Spaces inside brackets are mandatory: [ $x -eq 1 ] works but [$x -eq 1] does not.

short: Loop over a list or range
description: Repeats a block of commands for each item in a list, range, or set of files.
tip: Bash for loops are much more flexible than Windows batch. Loop over files with globs, sequences with {1..10}, or command output with $(command).

options.desc: Loop over lines in a file

example: # Windows: for %i in (*.txt) do echo %i for file in *.log; do echo "Processing: $file" gzip "$file" done # Numeric range: for i in {1..5}; do echo "Step $i" done

short: Loop while a condition is true
description: Repeatedly executes commands as long as a condition remains true.
tip: Use while true; do ... done for an infinite loop (exit with Ctrl+C or break). while read line is the standard pattern for processing a file line by line.
example: # Read file line by line: while read line; do echo "Line: $line" done < input.txt # Retry until success: while ! ping -c 1 google.com; do echo "Waiting for network..." sleep 5 done

short: Control flow — replace GOTO with functions
description: Bash has no GOTO. Use functions, break, continue, or restructure logic with loops and conditionals.
tip: There is no GOTO in bash — and that's a good thing. Restructure your logic using functions, which are cleaner and reusable.

options.desc: Return from function with exit code

example: # Windows: goto :label # Use functions instead: do_backup() { echo "Running backup..." tar -czf backup.tar.gz ~/Documents } do_cleanup() { rm -f /tmp/*.tmp } do_backup do_cleanup

description: Shifts positional parameters ($1, $2...) left by one. Used to process command-line arguments in scripts.
tip: Script arguments are $1 $2 $3 etc. $@ is all arguments, $# is the count. shift removes $1 and shifts the rest down.
example: #!/bin/bash # Script: myscript.sh arg1 arg2 arg3 echo "All args: $@" echo "Count: $#" echo "First: $1" # Process all args one by one: while [ $# -gt 0 ]; do echo "Arg: $1" shift done

short: Check command success or failure exit code
description: Reads the exit status of the last command. 0 means success; any non-zero value means failure.
tip: $? holds the exit code of the last command. Use && to chain commands that only run on success, and || for failure. set -e makes a script exit immediately on any error.

options.desc: Run cmd2 only if cmd1 succeeded
Run cmd2 only if cmd1 failed

example: # Windows: if %errorlevel% neq 0 echo Error ls /tmp echo $? # 0 (success) ls /nonexistent echo $? # 2 (error) # Chain commands: apt update && apt upgrade -y

description: Evaluates mathematical expressions in scripts or at the command line.
tip: Use $(( )) for integer arithmetic in bash — fast and built-in. For floating point maths use bc or python3 -c 'print(...)'.
example: # Windows: set /a result = 5 + 3 echo $((5 + 3)) # 8 echo $((10 * 7)) # 70 echo $((2 ** 8)) # 256 # Floating point: echo 'scale=2; 10/3' | bc # 3.33

description: Changes the title text shown in the terminal emulator's title bar.
tip: Terminal title escape codes work in most emulators (GNOME Terminal, Konsole, xterm). Add the command to your PS1 prompt definition in ~/.bashrc to update it automatically.
example: # Windows: title My Application echo -ne "\033]0;My Terminal\007" # Dynamic title showing current dir: echo -ne "\033]0;${USER}@${HOSTNAME}: ${PWD}\007" # Auto-update in ~/.bashrc: PROMPT_COMMAND='echo -ne "\033]0;${PWD}\007"'

short: View or modify the command search path
description: Shows or modifies the list of directories the shell searches when you type a command.
tip: PATH directories are colon-separated in Linux (not semicolons). ~/bin and ~/.local/bin are common places to put your own scripts — add them to PATH in ~/.bashrc.

options.desc: Check if command is in PATH
Clear command location cache after PATH change

example: # Windows: path echo $PATH # /usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin # Add a directory: export PATH=$PATH:~/bin # Make permanent (add to ~/.bashrc): echo 'export PATH=$PATH:~/bin' >> ~/.bashrc source ~/.bashrc

description: Deletes empty directories (rmdir) or directories with all their contents (rm -r).
tip: rmdir only removes empty directories. To remove a directory and everything inside it use rm -r. Add -v to see each file being deleted.
example: # Windows: rd /s /q MyFolder # Remove empty directory: rmdir emptydir # Remove directory and everything in it: rm -r MyFolder # Verbose — see what's being deleted: rm -rv MyFolder
gotcha: rm -rf with a wrong path can delete critical files. Double-check the path — there is no undo.

short: Create a file from keyboard input
description: Lets you type content directly into a new file from the terminal, without opening an editor.
tip: After cat > file, type your content and press Ctrl+D on a new line to save. For multi-line files in scripts, use a heredoc (<< 'EOF') instead.
example: # Windows: copy con newfile.txt cat > newfile.txt # type content, then Ctrl+D to save # Easier for single lines: echo 'Hello World' > newfile.txt # Multi-line using heredoc: cat > config.txt << 'EOF' host=localhost port=8080 EOF

description: Generates or checks checksums to verify that files have not been corrupted or tampered with.
tip: Always verify downloads using the checksum provided by the source. SHA-256 is preferred over MD5 as it is more secure. Many Linux ISO download pages provide a .sha256 file.
example: # Windows: certutil -hashfile file SHA256 # Generate: sha256sum ubuntu.iso a1b2c3d4... ubuntu.iso # Verify a download: sha256sum -c ubuntu.iso.sha256 ubuntu.iso: OK # Compare two files are identical: md5sum file1.txt file2.txt

description: Creates symbolic links (shortcuts) or hard links pointing to files or directories.
tip: A symbolic link (symlink) is like a Windows shortcut. A hard link is a second name for the same file data. Symlinks are far more commonly used and shown with ls -la using an arrow (->).

options.desc: Show what a symlink points to

example: # Windows: mklink link.txt target.txt ln -s /var/www/html ~/www # symlink to a directory ln -s /etc/nginx/nginx.conf ~/nginx.conf # symlink to a file # View what it points to: ls -la ~/www lrwxrwxrwx ... www -> /var/www/html
gotcha: Use absolute paths when creating symlinks to avoid broken links if the link is moved.

description: Views and sets fine-grained file permissions beyond the basic owner/group/other model.
tip: Basic Linux permissions (chmod) cover most needs. For Windows-style fine-grained ACLs, install the acl package and use setfacl.
example: # Windows: icacls file.txt /grant John:F # Install ACL tools: sudo apt install acl # View ACLs: getfacl /var/www/html # Grant a specific user access: setfacl -m u:john:rw /var/www/html

short: List open files and which process uses them
description: Shows which files, sockets, and devices are currently open and by which processes.
tip: lsof (List Open Files) is extremely powerful. In Linux, everything is a file — network sockets, devices, and pipes all appear in lsof output.
example: # Windows: openfiles /query # What process is using port 80? lsof -i :80 # What files does nginx have open? lsof -p $(pidof nginx) # Who has this log file open? lsof /var/log/app.log

short: Set or view disk volume label
description: Assigns a human-readable name (label) to a disk partition for easier identification.
tip: Labels appear in lsblk -f and can be used in /etc/fstab as LABEL=MyDisk for reliable mounting that survives device reordering.
example: # Windows: label D: MyDrive # View all labels: lsblk -f # Set label on ext4: sudo e2label /dev/sdb1 'DataDrive' # Set label on FAT/USB: sudo fatlabel /dev/sdb1 'MYUSB'

description: Creates a complete raw copy of an entire disk or partition — useful for backups, imaging, and forensics.
tip: dd is extremely powerful and equally dangerous — it writes raw bytes with no safety checks. Always double-check if= (input) and of= (output) before pressing Enter.

options.desc: Securely wipe disk with random data
Show live progress (add to any dd command)

example: # Clone disk to image: sudo dd if=/dev/sda of=~/backup.img bs=4M status=progress # Restore image to new disk: sudo dd if=~/backup.img of=/dev/sdb bs=4M status=progress # Write bootable ISO to USB: sudo dd if=ubuntu.iso of=/dev/sdb bs=4M status=progress
gotcha: dd is called 'disk destroyer' by some. Swapping if= and of= overwrites your source. Triple-check device paths before running.

description: Removes filesystem signatures from a disk or securely overwrites all data to prevent recovery.
tip: wipefs removes filesystem signatures quickly. shred overwrites data multiple times for a secure wipe. For SSDs, use the manufacturer's secure erase tool or hdparm --security-erase.
example: # Windows: diskpart → clean all # Quick wipe (removes signatures only): sudo wipefs -a /dev/sdb # Secure 3-pass wipe: sudo shred -vzn 3 /dev/sdb # Securely delete a single file: shred -u sensitive_data.txt
gotcha: shred is ineffective on SSDs and flash drives due to wear leveling — use the drive's built-in secure erase instead.

description: Recursively lists every file in a directory tree, one per line — no directories, just files.
tip: Combine with wc -l to count files, xargs to act on them, or grep to filter by name. -type d lists only directories instead.
example: # Windows: dir /s /b find . -type f # all files find . -type f | wc -l # count files find . -name '*.py' # python files only # Find and delete temp files: find . -name '*.tmp' -delete

description: Locates files modified within a specific time window.
tip: -mtime -1 means modified within the last 24 hours. -mmin -60 means within the last 60 minutes. Positive numbers mean older than that many days.

options.desc: List sorted by modification time (newest first)

example: # Windows: dir /od # Files changed in last 24 hours: find . -mtime -1 -type f # Changes in /etc in last 30 minutes: find /etc -mmin -30 # List with newest first: ls -lt

description: Searches text using extended regular expressions for complex pattern matching.
tip: grep -E enables extended regex with +, ?, |, and () without backslashes. Use grep -P for full Perl-compatible regex.
example: # Windows: findstr /r "error\|warn" file.txt # Match multiple patterns: grep -E 'ERROR|WARN|CRIT' app.log # Extract all IP addresses: grep -oE '[0-9]{1,3}(\.[0-9]{1,3}){3}' access.log # Config file without comments or blanks: grep -Ev '^#|^$' /etc/ssh/sshd_config

short: Display the first lines of a file
description: Shows only the first N lines of a file — useful for quickly previewing large files.
tip: head -1 shows just the header row of a CSV. Combine with grep for powerful one-liners. head -n -5 means 'all lines except the last 5'.
example: # No direct Windows CMD equivalent head server.log # first 10 lines head -n 50 server.log # first 50 lines head -1 data.csv # just the header row # Preview first 5 lines of every log: head -n 5 *.log

short: Display the last lines of a file
description: Shows the last N lines of a file. With -f, follows the file in real-time — essential for watching logs.
tip: tail -f is one of the most-used Linux commands for live log monitoring. Use it on multiple files at once — it labels each line with the filename.

options.desc: All lines except the first (skip header)

example: # No direct Windows CMD equivalent tail /var/log/syslog # last 10 lines tail -n 100 error.log # last 100 lines # Watch a log in real-time: tail -f /var/log/nginx/access.log # Follow multiple files: tail -f /var/log/nginx/*.log

short: Print file in reverse line order
description: Outputs a file with lines in reverse order — last line first. The name is 'cat' backwards.
tip: tac is handy for reading logs where the newest entries are at the bottom — reverse the file so the most recent entries appear first.

options.desc: Print file with last line first

example: # No direct Windows equivalent tac /var/log/syslog # newest entries first tac access.log | head -50 # last 50, most recent first

description: Removes or counts duplicate lines. Must be used after sorting — it only removes adjacent duplicates.
tip: uniq only removes adjacent duplicates — always run sort first. uniq -c adds a count prefix, making it great for frequency analysis.

options.desc: Show only lines that are duplicated

example: # No direct Windows CMD equivalent # Remove duplicates from a sorted list: sort names.txt | uniq # Top 20 IPs by hit count: awk '{print $1}' access.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -20 # Find duplicate lines only: sort file.txt | uniq -d

description: Replaces, deletes, or squeezes individual characters in text — a character-level find and replace.
tip: tr works on individual characters, not strings. For string replacement use sed. tr -d '\r' is the quickest fix for Windows line endings in text files.
example: # No direct Windows CMD equivalent # Uppercase: echo 'hello world' | tr 'a-z' 'A-Z' HELLO WORLD # Fix Windows line endings: tr -d '\r' < windows_file.txt > fixed.txt # Remove all punctuation: echo 'Hello, World!' | tr -d '[:punct:]'

short: Extract columns or fields from text
description: Cuts out sections of each line — by delimiter-separated field number or character position.
tip: cut -d ',' -f 2 extracts the second column of a CSV. For more complex field extraction, awk is more powerful and handles edge cases better.
example: # No direct Windows CMD equivalent # Extract usernames from /etc/passwd: cut -d ':' -f 1 /etc/passwd # Extract second column from CSV: cut -d ',' -f 2 data.csv # Extract IPs from log (column 1): cut -d ' ' -f 1 /var/log/nginx/access.log

short: Process and extract columns from structured text
description: A powerful text-processing tool that splits lines into fields and lets you extract, transform, and report on structured text.
tip: awk is a mini programming language. $1, $2, $3 refer to space-separated columns. $NF is always the last column. Incredibly useful for log files and CSV data.
example: # No direct Windows CMD equivalent # Print usernames from /etc/passwd: awk -F ':' '{print $1}' /etc/passwd # Print IPs from log (column 1): awk '{print $1}' /var/log/nginx/access.log # Sum a column of numbers: awk '{sum += $1} END {print sum}' numbers.txt

description: Converts binary data to Base64 text encoding and back. Common in APIs, certificates, and email attachments.
tip: Base64 encoding makes binary data safe to transmit as text. The output is about 33% larger than the input. Common in JWT tokens, embedding images in HTML, and API authentication headers.
example: # No Windows CMD equivalent (PowerShell has it) # Encode a file: base64 image.png > image.b64 # Encode a string: echo -n 'Hello World' | base64 SGVsbG8gV29ybGQ= # Decode: echo 'SGVsbG8gV29ybGQ=' | base64 -d

description: Clears locally cached DNS records, forcing fresh lookups on the next DNS request.
tip: The command varies by distro. On modern Ubuntu/Debian use systemd-resolve --flush-caches. Verify DNS settings with resolvectl status.

options.desc: View current DNS servers per interface

example: # Windows: ipconfig /flushdns # Modern Ubuntu/Debian: sudo systemd-resolve --flush-caches resolvectl flush-caches # alternative # Check DNS settings: resolvectl status cat /etc/resolv.conf

description: Releases the current DHCP IP address and requests a new one from the DHCP server.
tip: On desktop systems using NetworkManager, it's easier to use nmcli to disconnect and reconnect the interface rather than calling dhclient directly.
example: # Windows: ipconfig /release && ipconfig /renew # Direct dhclient: sudo dhclient -r eth0 # release sudo dhclient eth0 # renew # Via NetworkManager (desktop): nmcli dev disconnect eth0 nmcli dev connect eth0

short: View computers and shares on the network
description: Lists computers visible on the local network and shared resources on a specific host.
tip: For Windows shares, use smbclient -L //hostname. For discovering all live devices on the subnet, nmap -sn is the most reliable method.

options.desc: List Windows shares on a server

example: # Windows: net view # List shares on a Windows server: smbclient -L //192.168.1.10 -U john # Discover all live hosts on network: sudo nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24

short: View and sync system time via NTP
description: Shows time synchronisation status and synchronises the system clock with an NTP time server.
tip: Modern Linux uses systemd-timesyncd or chrony for automatic NTP sync. timedatectl shows whether sync is active and lets you set the timezone.

options.desc: Show NTP peers and sync status

example: # Windows: net time /set or w32tm /resync timedatectl # show current status timedatectl set-timezone Europe/London timedatectl set-ntp true # NTP server details: ntpq -p

short: Display MAC addresses of network interfaces
description: Shows the hardware (MAC) address of each network interface card.
tip: MAC addresses appear in ip link show on the line starting with link/ether. Each network interface has its own unique MAC address.

options.desc: Show all interfaces with MAC addresses
All MACs in one line each

example: # Windows: getmac /v ip link show 2: eth0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP> link/ether aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff # Just the MAC address: cat /sys/class/net/eth0/address aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff

win: netsh int ip set address (static IP)
description: Assigns a permanent static IP address to a network interface using NetworkManager.
tip: ip addr add sets an IP temporarily (lost on reboot). For a permanent static IP use nmcli on desktop systems, or edit /etc/netplan/ on Ubuntu server.
example: # Windows: netsh int ip set address 'Ethernet' static 192.168.1.50 255.255.255.0 # Permanent via NetworkManager: nmcli con mod 'Wired connection 1' ipv4.addresses 192.168.1.50/24 nmcli con mod 'Wired connection 1' ipv4.gateway 192.168.1.1 nmcli con mod 'Wired connection 1' ipv4.dns '8.8.8.8 1.1.1.1' nmcli con mod 'Wired connection 1' ipv4.method manual nmcli con up 'Wired connection 1'

description: Controls which network traffic is allowed through the system's firewall.
tip: ufw (Uncomplicated Firewall) is the easy-to-use front-end for Linux firewalls on Ubuntu/Debian. firewalld is used on Fedora/RHEL. Both sit on top of iptables/nftables.
example: # Windows: netsh advfirewall firewall add rule ... sudo ufw status # view rules sudo ufw enable # turn on sudo ufw allow ssh # allow SSH sudo ufw allow 80,443/tcp # allow web traffic sudo ufw status numbered # numbered list of rules
gotcha: Always allow SSH (port 22) before enabling the firewall on a remote server, or you will lock yourself out.

short: Combined ping and traceroute with statistics
description: Continuously monitors each network hop showing real-time packet loss and latency per hop.
tip: mtr is the perfect replacement for both pathping and tracert. Install it with sudo apt install mtr. Use --report for a non-interactive printout.
example: # Windows: pathping google.com mtr google.com # live updating route + stats mtr -n google.com # skip DNS for speed mtr --report google.com # non-interactive report # Install if missing: sudo apt install mtr

short: Network port scanner and host discovery
description: Scans networks and hosts to discover open ports, running services, OS versions, and security issues.
tip: nmap is the gold standard for network scanning. A simple nmap hostname shows open ports. Add -sV to detect service versions and -sn to just find live hosts.

options.desc: Scan common ports on a host
Ping scan — find all live hosts

example: # Install: sudo apt install nmap # Basic port scan: nmap 192.168.1.1 # Discover all live hosts: nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24 # Full scan with service versions: nmap -sV target.example.com
gotcha: Only scan networks and hosts you own or have explicit permission to scan. Unauthorised scanning may be illegal.

short: Log off the current user session
description: Ends the current user session, closing all running processes owned by that user.
tip: In a terminal, logout or exit ends the session. To forcibly log out another user, kill all their processes with pkill -KILL -u username.

options.desc: Terminate all sessions for a user

example: # Windows: logoff logout # in a login shell exit # in any shell # Force log out user 'john': sudo pkill -KILL -u john sudo loginctl terminate-user john

short: Hibernate — save state to disk and power off
description: Saves the current RAM state to disk and powers off. On next boot, the session resumes exactly where you left off.
tip: Hibernation requires a swap partition or swap file at least as large as your RAM. systemctl suspend (sleep) is faster and more commonly used on laptops.
example: # Windows: shutdown /h sudo systemctl hibernate # Sleep (suspend to RAM, faster): sudo systemctl suspend # Hybrid sleep (save to disk AND suspend): sudo systemctl hybrid-sleep
gotcha: Hibernation requires swap space at least equal to your RAM size and may not work on all hardware configurations.

description: Removes temporary files, old package caches, and other items that can safely be deleted to recover disk space.
tip: ncdu (interactive disk usage viewer) is excellent for finding what's consuming space before cleaning. Install with sudo apt install ncdu.
example: # Windows: cleanmgr (Disk Cleanup GUI) # Clean package cache: sudo apt clean sudo apt autoremove -y # Trim system logs: sudo journalctl --vacuum-size=200M # Find space hogs interactively: sudo apt install ncdu && sudo ncdu /

description: Shows total boot time and which services took the longest to start.
tip: systemd-analyze blame lists services sorted by startup time — perfect for diagnosing a slow boot. systemd-analyze plot generates a visual SVG timeline.
example: # Windows: msconfig → Boot tab # How long did boot take? systemd-analyze Startup finished in 2.1s (kernel) + 8.9s (userspace) # What's slowing it down? systemd-analyze blame 4.2s snapd.service 2.1s NetworkManager-wait-online.service

description: Updates the dynamic linker cache after installing new shared libraries (.so files) — the Linux equivalent of registering a DLL.
tip: When you install a library manually (not via package manager), run sudo ldconfig so programs can find it. Shared libraries go in /usr/lib/ or /usr/local/lib/.

options.desc: Check if a library is registered
Show which libraries a binary requires

example: # Windows: regsvr32 mylib.dll # After installing a .so library manually: sudo cp mylib.so /usr/local/lib/ sudo ldconfig # update the cache # Check it's registered: ldconfig -p | grep mylib # See what libs a binary needs: ldd /usr/bin/python3

short: See who is currently logged in
description: Lists currently logged-in users, what they are doing, and recent login history.
tip: w is the most informative — it shows not just who is logged in but the current command they're running and how long they've been idle.
example: # Windows: query user or net session who # john pts/0 Jan 15 09:00 w # who + idle time + current command last # login/logout history sudo lastb # failed login attempts

description: Defines a new system service by creating a unit file, then enables and starts it.
tip: Services are defined in plain text unit files. After creating or editing one, always run systemctl daemon-reload before starting or enabling it.

options.desc: View a service's unit file content

example: # Windows: sc create MySvc binpath= C:\app.exe # Create unit file: sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/myapp.service [Unit] Description=My Application After=network.target [Service] ExecStart=/usr/bin/myapp Restart=always [Install] WantedBy=multi-user.target sudo systemctl daemon-reload sudo systemctl enable --now myapp

short: Read BIOS and hardware serial numbers
description: Reads hardware information from the system BIOS/UEFI including serial numbers, model numbers, and firmware versions.
tip: dmidecode reads the DMI table from the BIOS — essential for finding serial numbers and warranty info without opening the case.
example: # Windows: wmic bios get serialnumber sudo dmidecode -s system-serial-number sudo dmidecode -s system-product-name # BIOS version: sudo dmidecode -t bios | grep -i version # RAM slots and speed: sudo dmidecode -t memory | grep -E 'Size|Speed|Type'

description: Shows battery charge level, time remaining, and health information on laptops.
tip: acpi -b is the quickest battery check. upower gives more detail including battery health percentage and charge cycle count.

options.desc: All ACPI info (battery, AC adapter, temp)

example: # Windows: powercfg /batteryreport # Quick battery check: acpi -b Battery 0: Discharging, 78%, 02:15:00 remaining # Detailed battery info: upower -i $(upower -e | grep BAT)

description: Displays temperature readings from CPU, GPU, and motherboard sensors.
tip: Run sudo sensors-detect once to auto-detect and configure which sensors are available, then use sensors to read them. watch -n 2 sensors updates every 2 seconds.

options.desc: All temperature and fan speed readings

example: # Windows: no built-in — use Core Temp or HWiNFO64 sudo apt install lm-sensors sudo sensors-detect # run once to detect sensors Core 0: +42.0°C (high = +86.0°C) Core 1: +41.0°C # Live monitor: watch -n 2 sensors

short: Display OS info with ASCII art logo
description: Shows system information in a visually attractive format — distro, kernel, uptime, CPU, RAM, and more.
tip: neofetch shows an ASCII art logo of your distro alongside system info. fastfetch is a faster modern alternative. Popular for sharing system specs in screenshots.

options.desc: Classic ASCII art logo + system info

example: # Windows: winver (GUI dialog) sudo apt install neofetch neofetch # Shows distro logo + OS, Kernel, Uptime, # Packages, Shell, CPU, GPU, RAM # Faster alternative: sudo apt install fastfetch fastfetch

short: Show how long the system has been running
description: Displays how long the system has been running since the last boot, along with current CPU load averages.
tip: Load averages show CPU load over the last 1, 5, and 15 minutes. A load equal to your core count means fully utilised. Higher means processes are waiting for CPU time.
example: # Windows: Task Manager → Performance → Up Time uptime 14:32:00 up 5 days, 3:41, 2 users, load average: 0.52, 0.48, 0.41 uptime -p # up 5 days, 3 hours, 41 minutes uptime -s # 2025-01-10 10:51:32

description: Lists hardware devices, their drivers, and status — the Linux equivalent of Device Manager.
tip: dmesg shows the kernel's hardware detection log — look here when a device isn't working. lspci shows PCI devices (GPU, NIC), lsusb shows USB devices.

options.desc: List PCI devices (GPU, network card, sound)

example: # Windows: devmgmt.msc (GUI only) lspci 00:02.0 VGA: Intel HD Graphics 01:00.0 Network: Realtek RTL8111 lsusb # list USB devices # Check for hardware errors: dmesg | grep -i 'error\|fail'

description: Opens a real-time interactive view of CPU, memory, disk, and network usage with process management.
tip: btop is visually impressive — it shows CPU, memory, disk, and network graphs together. htop is the classic choice and is almost always available. Both install via apt.
example: # Windows: Ctrl+Shift+Esc (Task Manager) sudo apt install htop htop F5=tree F9=kill F6=sort q=quit # Modern alternative: sudo apt install btop btop

description: Shows information about physical RAM modules including size, speed, type, and which slots are populated.
tip: This shows all DIMM slots including empty ones — useful for knowing if you can add more RAM and at what speed.
example: # Windows: wmic memorychip get capacity,speed sudo dmidecode -t memory | grep -E 'Size|Speed|Type|Manufacturer' Size: 16 GB Speed: 3200 MT/s Type: DDR4 # How many slots total: sudo dmidecode -t memory | grep -c 'Size:'

short: List programs set to run at startup
description: Shows all services and programs configured to start automatically when the system boots.
tip: Desktop autostart apps (visible in your session after login) are managed separately in ~/.config/autostart/ and /etc/xdg/autostart/.

options.desc: Check if a specific service is enabled

example: # Windows: wmic startup get caption,command # Enabled system services: systemctl list-unit-files --state=enabled # Desktop apps that start on login: ls ~/.config/autostart/ # Disable a slow startup service: sudo systemctl disable snapd.service

description: Installs a local .deb package file you have downloaded manually, rather than from a repository.
tip: Use apt install ./file.deb (with ./) rather than dpkg -i — apt automatically resolves and installs dependencies, while dpkg does not.

options.desc: Fix broken dependencies after dpkg install

example: # Windows: msiexec /i installer.msi # Download and install: wget https://example.com/app.deb sudo apt install ./app.deb # handles dependencies # Fix broken deps if you used dpkg -i: sudo apt --fix-broken install

description: Applies centralised configuration policies — the Linux equivalent of Group Policy Update.
tip: Linux has no built-in Group Policy. Enterprise environments use Puppet, Ansible, Chef, or Salt for policy-based configuration management.
example: # Windows: gpupdate /force # Puppet: sudo puppet agent --test # Ansible (local): ansible-playbook local.yml # View applied security policies: cat /etc/security/pwquality.conf

description: Linux stores all configuration in plain text files rather than a registry. System config is in /etc/, user config is in ~/.config/ and dotfiles.
tip: There is no registry in Linux — this is widely considered an advantage. All config is human-readable plain text. Edit with any text editor, and version-control with git.
example: # Windows: regedit (registry editor) # Key config file locations: /etc/hosts = hostname to IP mappings /etc/fstab = disk mount config /etc/ssh/sshd_config = SSH server settings ~/.bashrc = your shell aliases and PATH ~/.config/ = GUI app preferences # Edit system config: sudo nano /etc/hosts

description: Views or changes which application opens files of a particular type.
tip: On desktop Linux, file associations are managed through xdg-mime. Most users prefer to change them through the file manager GUI (right-click → Open With → Set as Default).

options.desc: Detect actual file type from content
Open file with its default app

example: # Windows: assoc .pdf or ftype pdffile xdg-mime query default application/pdf evince.desktop # Change default PDF viewer to Okular: xdg-mime default okular.desktop application/pdf # What type is this file? file unknownfile # ELF 64-bit / ASCII text / JPEG etc.

description: Compresses files or folders into a standard ZIP archive.
tip: The Linux zip utility is the closest match to PowerShell Compress-Archive. Use -r when the source contains directories.
example: zip archive.zip file.txt # create an archive containing one file zip -r project.zip project/ # archive a complete directory zip -9 -r backup.zip Documents/ # use maximum compression
gotcha: Existing archive entries may be updated rather than replaced. Delete the old archive first when you need a completely fresh result.

description: Extracts files from a ZIP archive into the current directory or a chosen destination.
tip: Use unzip -l to inspect an archive before extracting it, and -d to choose a destination directory.
example: unzip archive.zip # extract an archive here unzip archive.zip -d output/ # extract into a separate directory unzip -l archive.zip # preview the archive contents
gotcha: An archive can contain paths that overwrite existing files. Extract unfamiliar archives into a new empty directory.

description: Combines files and directories into a TAR archive without necessarily compressing them.
tip: tar is available on modern Windows and Linux, but Linux users commonly combine it with gzip or xz using -z or -J.
example: tar -cf archive.tar folder/ # create a plain TAR archive tar -czf archive.tar.gz folder/ # create a compressed tar.gz archive tar -cJf archive.tar.xz folder/ # create a highly compressed tar.xz archive

description: Extracts TAR, tar.gz, tar.xz and other tar-based archives.
tip: GNU tar usually detects compression automatically when extracting, so tar -xf works for many common TAR formats.
example: tar -xf archive.tar # extract a TAR archive mkdir -p output && tar -xf archive.tar.gz -C output/ # extract into a new directory tar -tf archive.tar.xz # preview archive contents
gotcha: Archives can contain absolute or parent-directory paths. Inspect untrusted archives with tar -tf before extraction.

description: Compresses individual files using gzip or xz to reduce their stored size.
tip: Windows compact uses transparent NTFS compression. gzip and xz instead create compressed files such as file.log.gz or file.log.xz.
example: gzip file.log # compress a file with gzip gzip -k file.log # compress while keeping the original xz -9 file.log # compress with maximum xz compression
gotcha: gzip and xz normally replace the source file. Use -k when you need to keep the original.

description: Restores files previously compressed with gzip or xz.
tip: Use the decompressor matching the file suffix: gunzip for .gz and unxz for .xz.
example: gunzip file.log.gz # decompress a .gz file gzip -dk file.log.gz # decompress while keeping the compressed copy unxz file.log.xz # decompress a .xz file

description: Creates 7z, ZIP and other supported archive formats with the 7-Zip command-line tool.
tip: The Linux package may be named 7zip, p7zip or p7zip-full, depending on the distribution.
example: 7z a archive.7z folder/ # create a 7z archive 7z a archive.zip folder/ # create a ZIP archive 7z a -mx=9 archive.7z folder/ # use maximum compression

description: Extracts 7z, ZIP, RAR and many other archive formats while preserving directory paths.
tip: Use 7z l to list contents first. The x action preserves folder structure, while e extracts everything into one directory.
example: 7z x archive.7z # extract an archive 7z x archive.7z -ooutput # extract into output/ 7z l archive.7z # preview archive contents

description: Creates a new empty file or updates an existing file timestamp.
tip: touch creates an empty file when it does not exist. When it already exists, touch updates its access and modification times.
example: touch notes.txt # create one empty file touch file1.txt file2.txt # create multiple files touch -d "2026-01-01 12:00" notes.txt # set a chosen timestamp

description: Displays detailed metadata such as file size, permissions, timestamps, owner and inode number.
tip: ls -l gives a quick summary, while stat provides the full metadata record for one or more paths.
example: stat filename.txt # inspect a file stat -c "%n %s bytes %a" filename.txt # show name, size and numeric permissions stat folder/ # inspect a directory

description: Converts a relative path or symbolic link into its normalized absolute path.
tip: realpath is useful in scripts when you need one canonical path. readlink -f provides similar behavior on GNU systems.
example: realpath ./notes.txt # print the absolute normalized path realpath ../Downloads # resolve a path containing .. readlink -f symlink # follow a symbolic link to its final target
gotcha: realpath normally fails when required path components do not exist. Use realpath -m on GNU systems to normalize missing paths.

short: Create a file of a chosen size
description: Creates an empty or preallocated file with a specific size.
tip: truncate changes the apparent file size. fallocate reserves real disk blocks and is better for testing storage allocation.

options.desc: Empty a file without deleting it

example: truncate -s 100M test.img # create a sparse 100 MB file fallocate -l 1G disk.img # allocate 1 GB of disk space truncate -s 0 logfile.txt # clear a file while keeping it
gotcha: Sparse files can appear large while consuming little disk space. Use du -h and ls -lh together to compare allocated and apparent size.

short: Map a directory to another path
description: Makes an existing directory tree accessible at a second mount point.
tip: Windows subst creates a drive-letter alias. Linux bind mounts expose a directory at another directory path instead.
example: sudo mkdir -p /mnt/project # create a mount point sudo mount --bind /home/user/project /mnt/project # map a directory to the mount point sudo umount /mnt/project # remove the mapping
gotcha: Bind mounts are not persistent after reboot unless added to /etc/fstab or recreated by a service.

description: Changes the user or group that owns a file or directory.
tip: Linux separates ownership from permissions. Use chown for owner/group changes and chmod for read, write and execute permissions.
example: sudo chown kris file.txt # set a new owner sudo chown kris:users file.txt # set owner and group sudo chown -R kris:users folder/ # change an entire directory tree
gotcha: Recursive ownership changes can affect many files. Verify the target path before using chown -R.

description: Copies and synchronizes directories efficiently, transferring only changed content when possible.
tip: rsync is the closest Linux equivalent to Robocopy. A trailing slash on the source changes whether the directory itself or only its contents are copied.
example: rsync -avh source/ destination/ # copy changed files and preserve metadata rsync -avh --progress source/ destination/ # show progress during the copy rsync -avh --delete source/ destination/ # remove destination files missing from source
gotcha: --delete permanently removes extra files from the destination. Run once with --dry-run before using it.

description: Writes new content to a file, replacing the previous contents.
tip: Shell redirection with > replaces a file. tee is useful when the destination requires sudo permissions.

options.desc: Replace a file with one line

example: printf "%s " "Hello" > file.txt # replace the file contents printf "%s " "one" "two" > file.txt # write several lines printf "%s " "setting=value" | sudo tee /etc/example.conf # write through sudo
gotcha: The > operator truncates the destination before writing. Use >> or tee -a when you intend to append.

description: Adds new text to the end of an existing file without replacing its current contents.
tip: Use >> for normal shell appends. Use sudo tee -a when appending to a file that requires elevated privileges.
example: printf "%s " "new line" >> notes.txt # append one line printf "%s " "new line" | tee -a notes.txt # append while echoing output printf "%s " "option=yes" | sudo tee -a /etc/example.conf # append with root privileges

description: Calculates a cryptographic checksum used to verify downloads and detect file changes.
tip: SHA-256 is a strong default for download verification. Compare the printed checksum exactly with the value published by the distributor.
example: sha256sum filename.iso # calculate a SHA-256 checksum sha512sum filename.iso # calculate a SHA-512 checksum sha256sum -c checksums.txt # verify a checksum list

description: Tests whether a file, directory or other path exists for use in shell scripts.
tip: The test command returns an exit status rather than printing true or false. In Bash, [[ ... ]] is usually easier to read.
example: test -e file.txt && echo "exists" # check whether a path exists [[ -f file.txt ]] && echo "regular file" # check for a regular file [[ -d folder ]] && echo "directory" # check for a directory

description: Prints one file or directory name per line without detailed metadata.
tip: The number 1 option forces one entry per line. Use -A as well when you want hidden files but not . and ...
example: ls -1 # show names only ls -1A # include hidden files ls -1 *.txt # show only text files

description: Displays recently discovered IP-to-MAC address mappings for devices on local networks.
tip: ip neigh is the modern Linux replacement for arp. Entries may be reachable, stale, delayed or failed depending on their current state.
example: ip neigh show # display the neighbor table ip neigh show dev enp5s0 # filter by interface sudo ip neigh flush all # clear cached neighbor entries
gotcha: Flushing the neighbor table can briefly interrupt local connectivity while entries are relearned.

description: Displays routes used to decide where network traffic should be sent.
tip: The route marked default is the normal gateway. Add -6 to inspect IPv6 routes.

options.desc: Show which route reaches a destination

example: ip route show # display the routing table ip -6 route show # display IPv6 routes ip route get 1.1.1.1 # trace the selected route to a destination

short: Show or change the computer name
description: Displays the current host name and, with administrator privileges, changes the persistent system host name.
tip: hostname prints the short name. hostnamectl also shows operating-system and kernel details on systemd systems.
example: hostname # print the current host name hostnamectl # show detailed host information sudo hostnamectl set-hostname workstation # change the persistent host name
gotcha: Changing the hostname may also require updating /etc/hosts for local name resolution.

description: Displays network interfaces and whether each link is up or down.
tip: ip link focuses on link-layer state. Use ip addr when you also need assigned IP addresses.

options.flag: sudo ip link set enp5s0 up

example: ip link show # list network interfaces ip link show enp5s0 # inspect a specific interface sudo ip link set enp5s0 up # enable an interface

description: Shows wireless interfaces, connection state, SSID, signal and other Wi-Fi information.
tip: nmcli is easiest on NetworkManager systems. iw dev provides lower-level wireless information.
example: nmcli device status # show all device states nmcli -f GENERAL,IP4,WIFI-PROPERTIES device show wlan0 # inspect Wi-Fi details iw dev wlan0 link # show the active wireless connection

description: Lists visible wireless networks, channels, signal strength and security information.
tip: NetworkManager may cache scan results. Add --rescan yes when you need a fresh scan immediately.
example: nmcli device wifi list # scan for wireless networks nmcli device wifi list --rescan yes # force a new scan nmcli -f SSID,SIGNAL,SECURITY device wifi list # show SSID, signal and security

short: Test a TCP port or web endpoint
description: Checks whether a remote host and port accept connections, or whether an HTTP endpoint responds.
tip: Use nc -vz for a raw TCP port test and curl -I for HTTP or HTTPS response headers.
example: nc -vz example.com 443 # test whether HTTPS is reachable curl -I https://example.com # request HTTP response headers timeout 5 nc -vz 192.168.1.10 22 # test SSH with a timeout

description: Looks up DNS records such as A, AAAA, MX, TXT and NS entries.
tip: dig provides detailed DNS diagnostics. Add +short when you only need the resolved value.
example: dig example.com # perform a DNS lookup dig +short example.com A # show only IPv4 answers dig example.com MX # query MX records

description: Makes HTTP requests, downloads files and inspects server responses.
tip: curl is ideal for APIs and custom requests. wget is convenient for recursive or unattended downloads.
example: curl -I https://example.com # inspect HTTP headers curl -LO https://example.com/file.iso # download a file wget https://example.com/file.iso # download using wget
gotcha: Review URLs before piping downloaded content directly into a shell.

description: Downloads files from HTTP, HTTPS or FTP sources from a script or terminal session.
tip: Use wget -c or curl -C - to resume a partially completed download.
example: wget -c https://example.com/large.iso # download with resume support curl -C - -LO https://example.com/large.iso # resume using curl wget -O output.iso https://example.com/file # save under a chosen name

description: Connects to a TCP host and port for simple protocol testing and troubleshooting.
tip: nc or netcat is a flexible replacement for Telnet when testing unencrypted TCP services.
example: nc example.com 80 # open an interactive TCP connection nc -vz 192.168.1.10 22 # scan one TCP port nc -l 9000 # listen for a connection
gotcha: Netcat traffic is normally unencrypted. Do not send passwords or sensitive data through a plain nc session.

description: Connects securely to a remote Linux, Unix or SSH-enabled system.
tip: SSH also supports tunnels, command execution and key-based authentication. Store host settings in ~/.ssh/config for convenience.
example: ssh user@example.com # connect to a remote host ssh -p 2222 user@example.com # connect on a custom port ssh user@example.com "uname -a" # run one command remotely
gotcha: Verify the host-key fingerprint before accepting a new server key, especially on untrusted networks.

description: Transfers files or directories securely between local and remote systems.
tip: scp uses SSH authentication. For resumable synchronization or large directory trees, rsync -e ssh is often better.
example: scp file.txt user@example.com:/tmp/ # upload a file scp user@example.com:/var/log/app.log . # download a file scp -r folder/ user@example.com:/srv/ # copy a directory

description: Captures packets from network interfaces for troubleshooting and protocol analysis.
tip: tcpdump is a command-line packet analyzer. Save captures as PCAP files when you want to inspect them later in Wireshark.

options.desc: Save packets to a PCAP file

example: sudo tcpdump -i any # capture all visible traffic sudo tcpdump -i enp5s0 port 53 # capture DNS packets only sudo tcpdump -i any -w capture.pcap # save a capture for later analysis
