gotcha: Packet captures can contain credentials and private data. Protect capture files and capture only networks you are authorized to inspect.

description: Shows proxy environment variables used by command-line applications and package managers.
tip: Linux proxy settings are often stored in http_proxy, https_proxy and no_proxy, but desktop environments and applications can also have separate settings.
example: env | grep -i proxy # find proxy variables printf "%s\n" "$https_proxy" # show the HTTPS proxy systemctl --user show-environment | grep -i proxy # check the systemd user environment

description: Provides a graphical interface for viewing disks, creating partitions, formatting volumes and managing mounts.
tip: GNOME Disks is convenient for routine drive tasks. GParted is stronger for partition resizing and offline maintenance.
example: gnome-disks # open the disk utility sudo gparted # open the partition editor lsblk -f # list disks and filesystems first
gotcha: Partitioning and formatting operations can permanently destroy data. Confirm device names and maintain backups.

description: Displays used and available capacity for mounted filesystems.
tip: df -h shows filesystem-wide usage. Use du when you need to identify which directories consume the space.
example: df -h # show free and used disk space df -h /home # check one filesystem df -i # check inode capacity

description: Shows block devices, filesystem types, labels, UUIDs and mount points.
tip: lsblk -f gives a tree of devices and filesystems. findmnt focuses on currently mounted filesystems.
example: lsblk -f # list volumes and filesystems findmnt # show mounted filesystems findmnt /home # identify the /home mount

description: Displays physical disks, model names, sizes, transport types and health information.
tip: lsblk lists hardware layout. Use smartctl when you need drive health and SMART diagnostics.
example: lsblk -d -o NAME,MODEL,SIZE,ROTA,TRAN # show physical disk details sudo smartctl -i /dev/sda # inspect one drive sudo smartctl -a /dev/sda # read SMART health data

description: Discards unused SSD blocks or defragments an ext4 filesystem when appropriate.
tip: Modern Linux filesystems rarely need traditional defragmentation. SSDs generally benefit from periodic TRIM, which systemd often schedules automatically.
example: sudo fstrim -av # trim supported SSD filesystems systemctl status fstrim.timer # check the scheduled TRIM timer sudo e4defrag -c /home # check ext4 fragmentation
gotcha: Do not run e4defrag on non-ext4 filesystems. TRIM support depends on the drive, filesystem and storage stack.

description: Creates, opens and closes LUKS-encrypted Linux block devices.
tip: LUKS with cryptsetup is the standard Linux full-volume encryption workflow and is conceptually similar to BitLocker.
example: sudo cryptsetup luksFormat /dev/sdb1 # create an encrypted volume sudo cryptsetup luksOpen /dev/sdb1 secure # unlock the volume sudo cryptsetup luksClose secure # close the unlocked mapping
gotcha: luksFormat permanently overwrites encryption metadata on the selected partition. Double-check the device path and securely store the recovery passphrase.

description: Lists mounted filesystems and attaches or detaches a filesystem from the directory tree.
tip: Linux mounts storage at directories rather than assigning drive letters. findmnt is the clearest read-only view.
example: findmnt # list current mounts sudo mount /dev/sdb1 /mnt/data # mount a partition sudo umount /mnt/data # unmount the partition
gotcha: Unmount a filesystem before disconnecting the device. A busy filesystem cannot be unmounted until applications release it.

description: Reads SMART health status and diagnostics reported by a physical disk.
tip: Install the smartmontools package to obtain smartctl. A passing summary does not guarantee a drive is healthy, so inspect attributes as well.
example: sudo smartctl -H /dev/sda # check the health result sudo smartctl -a /dev/sda # inspect all SMART attributes sudo smartctl -t short /dev/sda # start a short diagnostic test

description: Lists running processes or searches for processes by name.
tip: ps aux gives a complete snapshot. pgrep is faster when you only need matching process IDs.
example: ps aux # list all running processes pgrep -a firefox # find processes by name ps -eo pid,user,%cpu,%mem,comm --sort=-%cpu # show the highest CPU users first

description: Requests that a process exit or forcefully terminates it when necessary.
tip: Start with the default TERM signal. Use KILL only when the process does not respond because KILL prevents normal cleanup.
example: kill 1234 # stop one process gracefully pkill firefox # stop processes matching a name kill -KILL 1234 # force a process to end
gotcha: Killing the wrong process can end your session or destabilize services. Verify the PID and process owner first.

description: Launches a program in the background and optionally keeps it running after the terminal closes.
tip: A trailing & backgrounds a process. nohup also protects it from the terminal hangup signal.

options.desc: Start an application in the background
Keep a command running after logout
List jobs started by this shell

example: firefox & # start a background application nohup long-command > output.log 2>&1 & # detach a long-running task jobs -l # list shell jobs
gotcha: Backgrounding does not automatically make a production service reliable. Use a systemd service for persistent managed workloads.

description: Waits for a specified amount of time before continuing.
tip: sleep accepts suffixes such as s, m, h and d.
example: sleep 5 # pause for five seconds sleep 2m # pause for two minutes sleep 0.5 # pause for half a second

short: Run a command in the background
description: Starts a command without blocking the shell from accepting additional input.
tip: The shell prints a job number and process ID. Use jobs, fg and bg to manage shell jobs.
example: long-command & # start a background job jobs # list jobs in the current shell fg %1 # return a job to the foreground

description: Pauses a shell script until one or more background processes finish.
tip: A foreground command already blocks until it exits. Use wait specifically for jobs previously started with &.

options.desc: Start a job and save its PID
Wait for all current background jobs

example: long-command & pid=$! # start a job and capture its PID wait "$pid" # wait for one process wait # wait for every background job

description: Displays process IDs together with executable names and complete command lines.
tip: Customize ps -o to print exactly the fields needed by a script or troubleshooting session.
example: ps -eo pid,comm,args # list PIDs and command lines ps -eo ppid,pid,user,stat,comm # include parent process and state ps -p 1234 -o pid,etime,args # inspect one PID

description: Searches configured software repositories for packages matching a name or description.
tip: Choose the command for your distribution: apt on Debian/Ubuntu, pacman on Arch and dnf on Fedora.
example: apt search firefox # search Debian-based repositories pacman -Ss firefox # search Arch repositories dnf search firefox # search Fedora repositories

description: Displays packages currently installed through the system package manager.
tip: Package databases list software installed by that package manager. Flatpak, Snap and manually installed applications have separate inventories.
example: dpkg -l # list installed Debian packages pacman -Q # list installed Arch packages dnf list installed # list installed Fedora packages

description: Downloads and installs available updates from configured package repositories.
tip: Arch users should perform full upgrades with pacman -Syu. Avoid partial upgrades such as refreshing databases without completing the upgrade.

options.flag: sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade

example: sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade # upgrade Debian-based systems sudo pacman -Syu # perform a full Arch upgrade sudo dnf upgrade # upgrade Fedora

description: Downloads the latest package lists and repository metadata without necessarily installing updates.
tip: Refreshing metadata only checks what is available. Run the appropriate upgrade command afterward to install updates.
example: sudo apt update # refresh Debian or Ubuntu metadata sudo pacman -Sy # refresh Arch package databases sudo dnf makecache # refresh Fedora metadata
gotcha: On Arch Linux, do not install packages after pacman -Sy without completing a full pacman -Syu upgrade.

description: Lists installed packages for which newer repository versions are available.
tip: On Arch, checkupdates comes from pacman-contrib and safely checks updates without refreshing the live package database.
example: apt list --upgradable # list Debian-based updates checkupdates # list Arch updates dnf check-update # list Fedora updates

description: Installs a desktop application from a Flatpak repository such as Flathub.
tip: Flatpak applications use reverse-domain IDs such as org.mozilla.firefox. Search first when you do not know the exact ID.
example: flatpak search firefox # search Flatpak applications flatpak install flathub org.mozilla.firefox # install Firefox flatpak run org.mozilla.firefox # run the installed app

description: Lists installed Flatpak applications and runtimes.
tip: Use --app to hide runtimes and show only user-facing applications.
example: flatpak list --app # list Flatpak applications flatpak list --runtime # list supporting runtimes flatpak info org.mozilla.firefox # inspect one application

description: Uninstalls a Flatpak application and optionally cleans unused runtimes.
tip: After uninstalling applications, flatpak uninstall --unused removes runtimes that are no longer required.
example: flatpak uninstall org.mozilla.firefox # uninstall one application flatpak uninstall --delete-data org.mozilla.firefox # remove the app and its data flatpak uninstall --unused # clean unused dependencies
gotcha: --delete-data removes application data. Back up anything important before using it.

description: Provides a central catalog of Linux desktop applications distributed as Flatpaks.
tip: Flathub is widely used across Linux distributions. Applications can be installed from the web catalog or through graphical software centers.
example: flatpak remotes # list configured repositories flatpak search drawing # search the catalog flatpak install flathub com.github.maoschanz.drawing # install an application

description: Removes software installed through the distribution package manager.
tip: Use the same package manager that installed the software. Package names may differ from the application name shown in menus.

options.desc: Remove an Arch package and unneeded dependencies

example: sudo apt remove package # uninstall on Debian or Ubuntu sudo pacman -Rns package # uninstall on Arch and clean dependencies sudo dnf remove package # uninstall on Fedora
gotcha: Dependency-cleanup options can remove additional packages. Review the package manager transaction before confirming.

description: Shows loaded services and their current active, inactive or failed state.
tip: Add --all to include inactive loaded services, or use list-unit-files to inspect startup enablement.
example: systemctl list-units --type=service # list service units systemctl list-units --type=service --all # include inactive services systemctl --failed # show failed services

description: Starts a systemd service immediately for the current boot.
tip: Starting a service does not automatically enable it at boot. Use systemctl enable separately when needed.
example: sudo systemctl start sshd # start a service systemctl status sshd # verify its state sudo systemctl start nginx.service # start a named unit

description: Stops a running systemd service for the current boot.
tip: A service can be started again manually unless it is disabled or masked.

options.desc: Check whether it is still active

example: sudo systemctl stop nginx # stop a running service systemctl is-active nginx # check its state sudo systemctl mask nginx # block future starts
gotcha: Masking is stronger than disabling and can break dependent applications. Use it only when you understand the dependency impact.

description: Stops and starts a systemd service so configuration or runtime state is reloaded.
tip: Some daemons support reload, which applies configuration without a full restart or connection interruption.

options.desc: Reload or fall back to restart

example: sudo systemctl restart nginx # restart a service sudo systemctl reload nginx # reload configuration sudo systemctl reload-or-restart nginx # choose reload when possible

description: Controls whether a systemd service starts automatically during boot.
tip: enable --now both enables and starts a service. disable --now disables and stops it.
example: sudo systemctl enable --now sshd # start now and at boot sudo systemctl disable --now sshd # stop now and disable boot startup systemctl is-enabled sshd # check whether startup is enabled

description: Lists user accounts known through local files and configured identity services.
tip: getent respects NSS configuration, so it can include LDAP or other directory users in addition to local /etc/passwd entries.
example: getent passwd # list known users getent passwd kris # look up one account cut -d: -f1 /etc/passwd # list local usernames

description: Lists local and directory-backed groups known to the system.
tip: Use getent group NAME for one group, or groups USER to see a user’s memberships.
example: getent group # list groups getent group wheel # inspect one group groups kris # show a user’s group memberships

description: Creates a new local Linux user and optionally a home directory and login shell.
tip: adduser is an interactive helper on Debian-based systems. useradd is the lower-level command available broadly.

options.desc: Create a user with home and Bash

example: sudo useradd -m -s /bin/bash alice # create a user and home directory sudo passwd alice # set the user password sudo adduser alice # create a user interactively

short: Add a user to a group
description: Adds an existing user to a supplementary group.
tip: The -a flag is essential: it appends the group instead of replacing all existing supplementary memberships.
example: sudo usermod -aG wheel alice # add a user to wheel sudo usermod -aG docker alice # add a user to the Docker group groups alice # verify the result
gotcha: Omitting -a can remove the user from other supplementary groups.

description: Locks password-based login for a local account without deleting the account or its files.
tip: Locking the password may not block every authentication method, such as existing SSH keys or running sessions. Review the complete access policy when disabling an account.
example: sudo usermod -L alice # lock password authentication sudo passwd -S alice # check account password status sudo chage -E 0 alice # expire the account

description: Re-enables password authentication for a previously locked account.
tip: An expired account may remain unusable after unlocking. Check expiration with chage -l USER.
example: sudo usermod -U alice # unlock the account password sudo chage -E -1 alice # remove expiration sudo passwd -S alice # verify the account state

short: Run a command as another user
description: Executes one command or opens a login shell using another user account.
tip: Use sudo -u for one controlled command. Use su - USER when you need a full login shell and know the target user password or have root access.
example: sudo -u www-data id # run one command as another user sudo -u postgres psql # launch a program under a service account sudo su - alice # open another user’s login shell

description: Finds lines matching a string or regular expression in files or piped input.
tip: grep is case-sensitive by default. Combine -r, -i and -n for recursive, case-insensitive searches with line numbers.
example: grep "error" app.log # search one file grep -rin "todo" src/ # search a directory recursively journalctl | grep -i "failed" # filter another command

description: Prints new lines as they are appended to a log or other text file.
tip: Use tail -F for logs that may be rotated or recreated because it follows the filename rather than only the original file descriptor.

options.desc: Show the last 100 lines, then follow

example: tail -f app.log # watch new log lines tail -n 100 -f app.log # show recent history and continue following tail -F /var/log/nginx/access.log # survive log rotation

description: Counts lines, words, bytes or characters in text files and streams.
tip: wc -l is especially useful in pipelines when you need the number of matching records.
example: wc file.txt # measure a text file wc -l file.txt # count lines grep -i error app.log | wc -l # count search results

description: Shows line-by-line differences or compares two already sorted lists.
tip: Use diff -u for readable patches. Use comm when both files are sorted and you need unique or shared lines.

options.desc: Show lines unique to either list

example: diff -u old.conf new.conf # compare two text files diff -r folderA folderB # compare two directories comm -3 <(sort a.txt) <(sort b.txt) # compare sorted lists

short: Run a command for each input item
description: Consumes a list or stream and runs another command for each item.
tip: xargs -0 safely handles filenames containing spaces and newlines when paired with null-delimited input such as find -print0.

options.desc: Process one item at a time

options.flag: while IFS= read -r line; do printf "%s\n" "$line"; done < file.txt

example: printf "%s\n" one two | xargs -n1 echo # run a command for each item find . -name "*.tmp" -print0 | xargs -0 rm -v # handle filenames safely while IFS= read -r line; do printf "%s\n" "$line"; done < file.txt # process a file line by line
gotcha: Plain xargs splits on whitespace. Use null-delimited input for arbitrary filenames.

description: Keeps only lines or fields that satisfy a textual or numeric condition.
tip: Use grep for simple pattern matching and awk when conditions depend on fields or numeric comparisons.

options.desc: Filter by a numeric CSV field

example: ps aux | grep firefox # filter lines containing text awk -F, '$3 > 1000' data.csv # filter rows by a numeric field df -P | awk 'NR>1 && $5+0 > 80' # filter filesystem usage

short: Write command output to a file
description: Saves standard output to a file, either replacing or appending to it.
tip: Use > to replace, >> to append and tee when you also want to see the output on screen.
example: uname -a > system.txt # write output to a file date >> system.txt # append output ip addr | tee network.txt # display and save at the same time

description: Copies piped output both to the terminal and to one or more files.
tip: tee -a appends rather than replacing. It is also the standard way to write piped data through sudo.
example: ls -la | tee listing.txt # show and save output date | tee -a history.txt # append and display printf "%s\n" "value" | sudo tee /etc/example.conf # write through sudo

description: Parses JSON and selects fields, array elements or transformed output.
tip: jq is a dedicated JSON processor. Use -r when you want raw strings without JSON quotation marks.
example: jq . data.json # format and validate JSON jq -r ".name" data.json # extract one field jq ".items[] | select(.enabled == true)" data.json # filter JSON objects

description: Builds JSON values or reshapes existing JSON data from the command line.
tip: jq -n creates JSON without reading input. Use --arg to insert shell strings safely.
example: jq -n '{name:"Kris",active:true}' # create a JSON object jq -n --arg name "$USER" '{user:$name}' # create JSON from a shell variable jq '{name:.name,id:.id}' input.json # reshape existing JSON
